speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is August 13, 1996. This is an interview with Irene Boris. My name is Clifford Davids, and this is tape number two. Mrs. Boris, we were talking about the period of time between September '39 and June of '41 when the Russians occupied Kostopol. Toward the end of that period as the Germans were gearing up to invade, did you have any idea that things were going to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you become aware of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- you came aware because people were from Poland sending. And people used to, you know, like travel a little. I'm not going to say a lot. And you heard that in Poland starts to be ghettos. This time wasn't yet. And they kill-- start killing Jews and so. And a lot of them came. The last part of 19-- let's say, 1940. A lot of them went back. A lot of them came. But mostly in '41, the Russian army, the-- not the army, but the-- the big shots, there were a lot of Jews, a lot of Jews. And they said, you better go away if you want to be alive. They didn't know themselves, because the Russian army just-- they just stepped back. I don't know. I didn't see it too much. Maybe I saw it, but I didn't, you know, want to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you mentioned that the-- the people who were moving east told you about some of the killings that were going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: They--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They really didn't know yet about the killing. But they said that they had to go away, because they said if they'll stay longer, you know, the Germans were there. And they start little by little killing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they any more specific in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not yet the concentration camps weren't until, I guess, '41. So they didn't-- I don't think the people from the concentration camp went away. It's people who were outside. And they still had the-- the opportunity to cross the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people tend to not believe what they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What they were hearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody believed it, nobody. It was beyond anybody's-- they thought maybe they killed somebody who objected to maybe rich people. And like I said, my mother used to say, all the rich one are staying. There only one who going away are the poor people. What are they afraid for? It was your famous. And then she said, I'll rather die from Hitler's bullets than from Stalin's hunger. That was your famous expression, because the hunger was impossible. I mean, you know, you ate-- this is going to be later, I guess, when I was there. It was no food for weeks. And people died of starvation most of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But during the time that you were in Kostopol-- Kostopol--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you had sufficient food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your diet like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, whatever we could. We had a garden, so this helped us. And my mother was a very capable woman. And she used to go to the Ukrainian people and have a little potatoes and whatever they gave her. So I was comfortable being with my mother, because she was always the provider. She was always like watching out for us, that we didn't have to do nothing, just, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you going to school at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- from '39 to '41?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went. I went to-- to evening school. And then I went to learn how to work in a laboratory. It was compulsory to go and learn something.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: They made you to go to school to learn something. Because if you wanted to have like a ration and got bread and so, you have to-- they told you to go to learn, and you have to go. You obey them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there distribution lines for the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Distribution lines for food?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't mean going to a store. But did the Russians distribute food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Like today were, they're getting sugar. So everybody was staying two nights in a day in the line to have-- and then came to you are next, was no more sugar. So you didn't get it. They gave a pair of shoes. Size was no object, because you traded after. So if you catched the pair of shoes, it's fine. If your didn't, it's fine too. You stayed two days in line.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to get food or other items, other than through the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, unless you had somebody like-- and the villagers, the Ukrainians still had their-- you know, they used to be farmers. So if you had somebody who you know. But money wasn't important by that time. You had to trade with them. So my mother traded. She had a pair of shoes. She gave them her pair of shoes. She get some bread. She get some flour. And then my mother started-- it was some people who had a store from all kinds flour and all the things. And she-- and they were just boys. And she helped them with the cooking. And for money she didn't take, but she took some food home. So we weren't starving. I wouldn't say starving. But you had to fight for every little thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from '39 to '41, your family managed to survive. And it--

speaker: interviewee
text: You survived. You didn't live. You survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of families that left during that period of time, a lot of Jewish families that left?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left. A few young people, the Russians started having like a [SIGHS] campaign, that who wants to go and work for Russia there. It's good and everything. So they left, quite a few. I don't know, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't stay in touch with those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family was not interested at that time to go into Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: As the-- as the months approached up until approximately June of 1941, were the-- were the Russians becoming more hostile towards you? Or did it become apparent that there was going to-- that change was in the air?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You didn't feel it unless you knew somebody who was in the higher rank of the Russian army. So you felt, you know, that something was going to be on. But they didn't believe themself. It was just like a-- all of a sudden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And over that period of time, were there a steady stream of refugees coming from the east?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those who settled in Kostopol, what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, because it-- it was just like-- like they're coming here, illegal immigrants. And you talk about them. And that's how they talked about them. They coming in, and they taking away this. A lot of single boys came in and they got married, so they-- one looked into our family. And it wasn't easy. It wasn't an easy thing. I don't know where they worked. I was too young, really, to-- maybe I wasn't so young in years, that I was young in my mind, that I didn't-- I wasn't exposed to the everyday living like some other girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first find out that the Germans had invaded Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it started being normal, you know? The army start being present, the Russian army in the city. And then they came up in one night. And then they started making like they came up to our house and says, this house, you know? That was when the Germans came in. They'll need this house. So we had to vacate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: But before-- you mean before the Germans came in? The Russian army, really, in this day, they came up and they were telling you that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that things sounds so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So initially, you had more Russian troops coming into the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they-- it was the one they-- from the other parts who are nearer to the border. They start marching back. And you saw-- I mean, it was present just of Russian troops. Nobody went into the outside, you know? Most of the people were hiding in their houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of plans was-- was your family making?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody had any plans, nobody. Just one night, next door was a very rich man. They had a leather good store, and they had other business. They used to-- in-- of the day I was leaving, a Russian [INAUDIBLE] some, you know? And he says that he came up and he said, the young people should really go away from the city. And even they called up on my brother. And he says, he should. Szloma was his name. In the meantime, the Russians took away my brother to-- to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Szloma?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was [? Abe ?]. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham.

speaker: interviewer
text: Abraham.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They took him to the-- and then he went to the Russian army. They took him, and then to the Polish army. And then they took him-- he was, I mean, he was always with that involved, you know, them at work. And then they came. They said to my brother Szloma, he should go away, because he's a young boy. So this time, I says I'm going too. I don't know. I don't remember, really, or maybe just I don't want to remember how we went. And we start hiding, because the Ukrainians, some of them, they came up, and they says, you have to give us this. You have to give us this. And this house, because we had such a big house, was in the center, because the synagogue. This is going to be-- and they said that this is going to be the ghetto. And they start moving in, just like this, without you want it or you don't want it. I came, [SIGHS] and it was in the dining room. We had beautiful furniture, because one of my mother first cousins had a furniture, like a factory. And they stayed in our house for a while, and my mother catered to them. So they made us nice furniture. And I took the tablecloth and start putting in a few dresses and those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then a girlfriend of mine came in. And she lived next to it. And she says, how can you leave everything and go? You just can't leave everything we've worked all our lives and go? And I said, it's up to you. And then I start packing just a few dresses. I don't remember what I had then. My mother start crying. She says, where are you going? I'm-- I says, can't you see? They all around the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just Ukrainian, not the-- the Germans didn't go yet exactly to the houses. And I says, no. I'm going. And that's how I packed. So she packed a few pieces of leather, mostly, you know, of give me-- to fix it and this. And she says, we're going too. And then my brother, the same thing. And that's how we started going. Nowhere, we didn't know where we're going and what we're going to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was yourself. It was Szloma and your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time, were the Russians still in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians were half and half, you know? Some of them-- most of the one they stayed there. They were in the houses. Nobody walked out. And the army was going back. The army wasn't already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Ukrainians that you mentioned, who were very attached to? Were they part of--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Germans. To the Germans. Most of the time, most of the time. If it's one good one, he hide it. That was one who wanted to take me in to stay with him. But he didn't want my mother, because my brother. So-- and I just didn't want. I wasn't interested. I didn't know why. It's just that something. It's destiny. And I'm still saying everything is destiny. It meant to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the first one in your family to acknowledge the fact that you needed to leave the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Between the time you made the decision and started packing dresses, how much time passed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't pack . Maybe a half hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what day that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, if I'm not mistaken, it was a Wednesday. I don't remember. I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in June sometime, 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the end. I-- you know, it's funny. Maybe I don't want to remember. I just don't remember dates, because this time, a date was-- you know, nobody cared the date and the day. It was like any other day. An we start-- but first we stopped in a place. And when they came there, were other people they are hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- let me-- let me stop you there. So what time of the day did you actually leave your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you made a decision the night before to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not before, in the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next door neighbor came up, and it says, you know, the Germans are here. And they-- you know? They thought they should send away my brother. He should go with them. But I didn't want to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans actually entered the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: That night?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know that we-- we saw them. But I don't know we-- we made anything out of them, you know? It was like such a [INAUDIBLE], such a turmoil, the Russian army, the Ukrainian, the Jews, the-- the morning they came in. It was too much of a turmoil. And they didn't start from the first day, you know? You-- you didn't go across them. They were more interested to establish in the city than, you know, look for us. This was in the later part.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're telling me that between the time the Germans arrived and the time you left was less than 24 hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a little bit more than 24 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately how long was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days, yeah. But then I was still in the-- in the vicinity. But I wasn't in our house, because one came in, like I said, and says that this house is going be, you know, has to be vacant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that morning you left the house--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you-- did you see any Germans on your way outside of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't see them in the city because we went to the Ukrainian part of Kostopol. And from there, we walked. I mean, was not transportation, so we walked. And we walked over to a a smaller town than ours, but far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ludwipol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-U-D-W-P-O-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the road between Kostopol and Ludwipol did you encounter any Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: We encountered them after Ludwipol. It was the time the Germans flew very low. And they dropped the parachutes. The army, they dropped from parachutes, most of them did. So we saw them. And they were just laid out in the woods after we went to Ludwipol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me-- let me just go back a little bit. So when you left the house, did you have any idea where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely not.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you headed east?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went because some other people been there. And you just follow the crowd.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you arrived in that small village, where did you stay that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was one house. And there were maybe 1,000 people or maybe less, you know? Time time, you didn't look so. And we stayed overnight. And in the morning, you walked again without any destiny. You didn't know where you're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't sleep. Who-- who needed sleep?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing too. I guess, maybe, my mother took her bread with her, you know? I don't know. Food wasn't important. You didn't eat for-- for days and days and days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened that next morning after you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked, not knowing where. And we came up to a place. And the airplanes were just on top of you. And it was like one side was the woods, and it was the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were laying down in the woods, and thought, you know, the airplanes going to-- and the parachutes, the army, they dropped in the thousands and thousands of Germans. But they were so busy they didn't bother with us. They were busy, I guess, with the-- I don't know. I don't know what they were busy with. So you saw the German paratroopers on the second day after leaving. You saw them--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --dropping from the skies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, in the hundreds and hundreds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a contract with any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, absolutely no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you hide in the woods during the day and then travel at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: We hide-- we-- during the day. And in the night, we just walked in the woods, not knowing any-- maybe one was a little more knowledgeable where he goes. But you just walked. You didn't had no choice. You had to go with the crowd.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large-- how large of a group were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably about 50 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you walk through the woods for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say it was more than-- it was about two weeks you walked till we came-- I don't remember the city, but it was in Russia. And my mother spotted a-- a train going. And soon as she spotted a train-- she was-- she was a young woman at the time. And she went in. And we had not suitcases, but a little, you know, whatever it was in the hand we took.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we went in on the train. And my mother took me in through the window. And the-- the train was covered with all kind of greens, you know, because-- and we didn't know where it going. We didn't know. And we just on the train. That's it. It wasn't a train. It was mostly like cattle, you know? The-- what they call it? Cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, cattle car, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survive while you were in the-- those two weeks while you were in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't, you know-- if somebody had a piece of bread, you shared. You did share. People were very sharing, yeah, because everybody thought that's the last day of their life. But some-- I would say, in Russian, some of the farmers, they bringed out, not too much, but whatever they had, they-- they brought up a little bit of food. So natural, everybody had a-- just a crumb.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pay for your-- your train fare when you got on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, who had money? No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did the train drop you off? Or where did you get off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went up the first, we stayed in Stalingrad, near Stalingrad. It was a place-- a fish, you know, on the Volga. We stayed there a few weeks and worked, you know, whatever. We catched fish. I mean, you don't have to catch them. You just, you know, took them. And then the Germans came there. You hear that they're coming. So I didn't stay again. We just went. And luckily, we got another train. And you didn't know where you're going. Nobody knew, absolutely no. They're just cattle trains. But this time, was a-- a farmer there. And he gave us a little bit too of some bread or so. So we took with us. And we went on the train. And we traveled a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not knowing when, not knowing where.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with any of the group that you had started off with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely no.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just you, your brother, and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried-- my mother tried to keep us together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of the Russians who helped you along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I tried after that war-- I tried to write a letter to a Russian family in-- I had the answer that the Germans killed your husband. They killed your daughter. And she's all alone. And that's all. And I lost-- I mean, I lost my memory by the time, you know-- the names and so, I don't remember, absolutely no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose husband and daughter were they referring to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Russian couple, the Russian-- the farmers where they kept us a few days there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you continued to head east, just ahead of Hitler's armies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sometimes we went with them, you know? Like we were in the woods, and they were in the city, you know? And we were just-- this time, they didn't bother yet to. They had their goal, you know, to capture in the Russian army. So-- so they didn't bother with the people who walked, were in the woods or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this time was not partisans yet. They tried to form, you know? That people who were hiding in the woods, and in the woods, you said it was the summer. So you had like blueberries, some of them. They had some, whatever, you know? And you tried to live on it. What did you do for drinking water?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So pick up now. You're-- you're still-- last time you had been traveling on the train for two weeks. And where did you end up after that particular part of the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, more than two weeks, the second time, more than two weeks. We-- the train stopped in-- in Tashkent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tashkent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And it stopped in Tashkent, and they said, who wants to go out? That's how far we're going. It wasn't forcible. You were on your own. That's the place we stopped. And if you wanna, you have to go out and look for something else. We stopped. And we slept maybe a few hundred people. We stand up in one room with-- And then they came up and they said that they have-- they're going to take us to cotton plantations to work on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some Russian people, Russian people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they government officials?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They must be. I don't know. I don't know. So they said if you want to stay here, you have to work on the cotton plantations. Otherwise, you're on your own. We had nothing. We had no bread, no food, no nothing. Even water then wasn't there. So we went with them. And they took us to a place on a plantation of cotton. And we stayed there because the first thing, we had no documents, nothing there from Russia. The second thing, the Germans were still coming further in Russia. So nobody bothered with nobody. They gave you a piece of bread once in two weeks. And before your time came in to go to this-- you know, to get your rationing, they were out of bread. So there was nobody you could say anything. It was, you know, a chaos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about the hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: The hunger was bad. People died of hunger more than of anything else, because it was hunger, no-- no soap. The dirt was awful. And over there, the water, it was coming from the mountains with all the dirt. And they using like the water came from my house. They had a canal to your house. You washed and everything in this. And you drink the water. So that's why they always used to boil the water there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a lot of disease and sickness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much, very much. It was diarrhea, malaria, typhus. Anything just mentioned, they had there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could you possibly do for medical care on your journey from--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had medical care. I didn't inquire till I was in Namangan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It N-A-M-A-G-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was where the cotton plantation was located?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cotton plantation was near there, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was in Uzbekistan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm sorry. Please go head.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and when you need it, you had to go into the city. They had a, like a doctor, I would say. It's really a nurse practitioner. They had in-- in every plantation, they had a nurse practitioner. But the hospitals were all in the city. You had to go into the city. You didn't have to pay. And the hospital had a place they took you. To say that it was my mother had gangrene. They operated on her without anesthesia, because they didn't have any. I don't blame them. It was the time that the Germans were just pushing more and more. So whatever they did, I think they did more than-- than anybody would do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when did you arrive at the cotton plantation?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was-- I don't know. It was in the 19-- this was 1941, the end of '41 or the beginning of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: So sometime in the beginning of the winter of 1941, '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. This was in the beginning of the winter, because the winters there aren't long. But they still have a little of snow, a little of freezing. And then they have-- the ground is mostly made of-- not-- what they call it-- that you can sink in it, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mud?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mud, mostly mud, yellow mud.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do on a cotton plantation in the middle of the wintertime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, over there, in the winter, you took out the-- the cotton grows on some-- whatever it is there, the-- and you have to take them out. And you use that for-- for wood, to-- to cook with it. That's what they using, the natives there. They use this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was what you did when you first arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in the winter. We used to take out the-- the sticks. And if they let you, take a few home and I-- and the rest for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mrs. Boris, we're going to change tapes now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine.
